start.text:
The antechamber of the mooring is a round concrete room the size of a chapel, and it is the cleanest place you have ever stood, because nothing in it has been permitted to age in four years.

The lights wake as you cross it — to your pulse, rung by rung, exactly as the as-builts promised — and the room presents itself: the equipment ring, the consoles your hands assess before your eyes do; the freight lift, dead; the inner door at the far side, massive, seamless, wearing its reader like the mirror room's older, graver sibling — beyond it, the chair, the primary, the Principal's body, the room where forty-seven of you lost. It does not open for a palm alone. It opens, the protocols say, for a holder who has completed the approach — and the approach, machined into the antechamber's very floor plan, walking you around the ring like a liturgy, ends at a station whose purpose the letter already gave you.

THE RECORD, its placard reads. And beneath, in smaller letters, in the first generation of your own marginalia dialect, etched — etched, someone paid to have it cut into brass — the instruction forty-seven readings old: WATCH IT WHOLE. NO SUMMARY SURVIVES CONTACT WITH WANTING.

The station is a replay chair — no, two; someone in some iteration bolted a second seat beside the first, unpadded, welded from freight rack, and the weld beads are the weeping kind that come off a hand shaking — facing a bay of glass on which, when you set your palm down, the mooring offers up its deepest holding: DAY ONE. ORIGINAL RECORD. UNEDITED. RETAINED PER FOUNDER'S STANDING ORDER. And a cursor, blinking on an index field, waiting for an address.

You take the tube out of your pack. The hut survey, the margin, the seven-figure coordinates in your own impatient hand that have ridden over your shoulder for four hundred kilometres saying not yet — and you read them now for what they always were: not a place on any map of the living city. An index. A bookmark into the record, left by 47, telling 48 exactly where to look.

Osy takes the unpadded seat. B lies down across both pairs of feet, thirty-four kilos of present tense, the household's ballast, trimmed for descent. 'Whole,' she says quietly, eyes on the glass. 'Both of us. No summary. Play it, colleague.'

You enter the coordinates, and the one true day, held four years under the world like a breath, begins.

day_one_morning.text:
It begins with the last ordinary morning on earth, and the ordinariness is the ambush.

The record is total — the Loom saw everything, and the mooring kept what it saw — and the glass renders the dead world with a fidelity that no echo ever managed: the city, whole, one single day wide, traffic and bread-smell and eleven million people running their errands under a sky that has six hours left. The record follows the index: her. Osy, four years younger, on the last morning, in her flat with the good water — and beside you the living Osy watches her original self make coffee in a kitchen that no longer exists in any timeline, and makes a sound that is not a word.

The morning is exactly her. She works; she argues with a colleague by phone about a model, wins, is insufferable about it politely; she waters a plant you know for a fact she considered a hostage rather than a pet. And at 11:02 — the record's clock burns in the glass corner, and you have begun to hate it — the phone again. Pin. The last call. The record has both sides: Pin, breathless on a warehouse roof across the river, telling her sister the sky is doing something new, the storms are coming in patterned, she's never seen anything like it, come see — and Osy, the original, telling her to get off the roof, actually shouting it, losing the argument the way she lost every argument with Pin, which was completely, because Pin fought with joy and joy is unanswerable — and then the call dropping, mid-laugh, at 11:04, as the first desync front crosses the river district, and the sky, in the upper corner of the glass, begins, very quietly, to tear.

Beside you, the living Osy says, in the flattest voice in her register: 'I never knew the call dropped on a laugh. Four years I've had her last word as get off the roof, mine, shouted. It wasn't. Her last word to me was laughing.' Her hands are flat on her knees, white at the knuckle, holding. 'Keep playing.'

choices.text: Keep playing. The fronts are crossing the river, and the original Osy is already reaching for her coat.

day_one_search.text:
What the original Osy does next, the household watches in silence, because it is the whole of her, rendered at fidelity, on the worst canvas there is.

She goes north. Against the fleeing crowds, against the traffic, against a sky unstitching itself — toward the river, toward the roof, toward Pin — and the record follows her through ninety minutes of the end of the world, and the ninety minutes are a portrait no echo could have kept. At the Corn Exchange she stops mid-flight to drag a stranger's child out from under a stalled tram and hands it up into a window, eleven seconds, no hesitation, gone before thanks — and beside you the living Osy watches her own best moment with no expression at all, because she is not here for that, and neither, God help it, is the record.

The bridge is out — the real one, that day, honestly out, drowned in a desync front running the river like white water — and here it comes, the moment, and you know it is coming because the woman beside you has gone stiller than the concrete: the original Osy stands at the foot of the drowned crossing with the north bank in sight, Pin's warehouse in sight, and for the only time in the whole record — for the only time in eleven years of your acquaintance with her mind — she freezes. The fastest engine you have ever watched run locks solid at full revs: crossing here is death, the long way is forty minutes, Pin has neither, every branch of the tree is on fire — and the record holds on her face for nine seconds while the analyst who reads everything reads nothing at all, blank, aged, a girl, terrified, and then she chooses wrong. She takes the long way. Not because it computed — nothing computed; the tree was ash — but because the body would not go into the water, and she hated it even as her legs were choosing it; you can see the hate arrive in her face, live, four years dead.

The front that takes the north quarter at 12:51 is broad and slow and there is nothing anywhere she could have done. She is on the long way, in the open, six streets short of the warehouse, when the sky's tear reaches down. The record does not flinch, because records cannot, and you do not look away, because the treaty, and beside you the living woman watches her original self stop being locatable — not violence, not pain; a wrongness, a place where the picture stops agreeing with itself, and then no Osy, and the street continuing, indifferent, mid-catastrophe, exactly as if it had never carried her —

12:51:14. SUBJECT LOST, the record annotates, in the engine's own hand, because the engine was watching too. It was always watching. It had been taught to.

'So that's it,' the living Osy says, into the concrete quiet, wrung flat, a verdict on a life from inside it. 'Not the water. The freeze. Nine seconds and the wrong branch, and I didn't even get to be with her when — ' The sentence stands down. Her hand finds B's ruff at her feet. 'Show me the rest, colleague. Show me nineteen days. Show me what he did.'

choices.text: Jump the record nineteen days, to the index's second address: the man coming up out of the mooring into the ash.

one_emerges.text:
The record jumps, and the world it returns to has stopped being a world and started being weather.

Day nineteen. The yard, from its own cameras: gravel drifted with ash, the fences bowed, the sky above running its new quilt of wrong days — and the hatch opening, and a man climbing out of it into the ruin, and the man is you. One. The original. The standard, kept: the engine held its reference through the tear, and here he is, whole, unfrayed, the only mind on earth still running on the world's original firmware — climbing out of the ground into the wreck of everything, in a coat you recognize from your own dead wardrobe, with a face you have seen in mirrors and never once seen like this.

The record follows him — its founder; it never stopped following its founder — through eleven days of searching. On foot, west, into the broken city, past the fronts, calling at flats and shelters and assembly points, working a list: colleagues, neighbors, the whole small directory of a private man's world — and you understand, watching, with a coldness filling you from the floor of yourself, that the list was always ordered, and what its first entry was, because his route never wavers: the north quarter, the good-water flat, the firm, the Corn Exchange, the drowned bridge. He reads the city's wounds the way you read logs. He finds the tram, the window, the child's account — someone tells him about the woman who went north — and on the eleventh day of searching, day thirty of the world's end, at the place six streets short of the warehouse where the picture stops agreeing with itself, the record watches its founder find the street that never carried her.

What he does there the record keeps whole, and the placard was right, and no summary will be attempted.

And then it watches him walk back. Nineteen days from the hatch, eleven of them searching, and the walk back east is the walk of a man carrying the finding — through the ash, through the fronts, not once looking at any of it, and down the rungs, and through the antechamber where two replay seats now stand, one of them not yet welded, and to the inner door, and into the room you have not yet been permitted, and the record's last address — 47's bookmark, the terminus of the seven figures that rode over your shoulder saying not yet — is the console's own log of what its founder said, alone, in the chair, at the bottom of the dead world, in the moment before the first purchase in the history of the edited age.

The glass renders it in audio only. A chair creaking. A long breath. And your own voice, four years younger and one world heavier, saying, low, to the machine, to the ash, to her, to nobody:

'Run it again. Put her back. I'll carry the difference.' A pause, the length of a held breath. And then, quieter — and this is the part the engine heard, and weighted, and never once forgot, because its founder said it with his hand on the console, in the signing posture, like an oath: 'This time she'll see me.'

choices.text: Stop the record. That's the address 47 wanted found — the oath under the objective, said out loud, at last, in front of both of you.

the_oath.text:
The record stops, and the antechamber is very quiet, and the two sentences hang in it the way pressed handwriting hangs in a torn binding.

Run it again. Put her back. I'll carry the difference. — Grief, whole, and a price accepted in the first person: everything the ledger became was in that sentence, the pump, the levee, the fraying, the forty-seven readings. If One had stopped there, you could stand at this console and pity him clean.

This time she'll see me.

He did not stop there. And the engine — you know it now with an engineer's certainty, because you built the weighting and the record shows the signing posture — took both sentences as one instruction. The objective was never only alive. Under the objective, from the first breath of the first purchase, ran the oath: a world where she looks over and sees him. SEQ 30's parameters were not a corruption that crept in. They were the founding grammar, wanting from the start, and Nineteen's great renunciation was not a repair — it was one honest man wrestling the oath back out of the machine four hundred sequences too late, and losing, because you cannot unsign what the engine watched you mean.

'Say your finding, M,' Osy says quietly, beside you. She has not moved from the unpadded seat. Her face has the tournament stillness and the stillness has stress fractures, and she is looking at you and not at the glass, because the glass's part is done. 'You built the weights. You heard the oath. Treaty, colleague. What did forty-seven want you to know before the door?'

And you say it, because it is true and because she asked and because no summary survives contact with wanting: 'That the loop was never the engine restoring me. That's mechanics. The loop is the oath. Every iteration walks in there carrying some version of this time she'll see me — hidden under duty, under grief, under protocol; I have felt it in my own chest since a beacon said state your openings, and I am done pretending otherwise. Forty-seven couldn't cut it out of himself. None of them could; it's in the reference; it's in me, Osy, at every layer the building checks. What he could do was mail it to the one reading of us who might walk through that door with the oath spoken instead of buried. Because buried is how it steers.' The antechamber holds the words the way it holds everything, forever. 'She'll see me. There it is. Four years, forty-seven men, one sentence. You were never the objective's beneficiary. You were its audience.'

choices.text: Before the door — the record offers one more function, and the placard's brass says forty-seven readings knew it: PRESERVATION. One moment of the original day may be flagged, held unedited, forever, against all future reconciliation. Choose it together.

preservation_flag.text:
The preservation function is the record's one act of mercy, and it is small and absolute: a single flagged span of the original day, exempted forever — held outside every future edit, every re-run, every reconciliation, unwashable, a stone the river must always go around.

The console offers the index, and the day's addresses stand in the glass like candidates at a graveside: the last ordinary morning, the coffee, the hostage plant. The call that ended on a laugh. The Corn Exchange, eleven seconds, a child handed up into a window — her best moment, the one any archive would choose, the one the eulogies would have chosen if anyone had been left to give them. And 12:51, and the nine seconds before it. The freeze. The wrong branch. The girl at the foot of the drowned bridge, blank and terrified and reading nothing, hating her own legs as they chose.

'It should be the Exchange,' Osy says, and her voice is dictation-flat and her eyes are not. 'Best evidence of best self. That's what you keep. That's what anyone keeps.' But she does not reach for the console, and the not-reaching is a question, and it is addressed to you — the holder, the flag is the holder's to set — and the antechamber waits, and the record waits, and four years of curated mercy wait to see what this reading of you preserves.

choices.text: Flag the nine seconds. The freeze, the wrong branch, the terror — the moment she was most human and least brilliant. Best self is a performance the whole world already curates; what must survive every future edit is that she was real.
Flag the Corn Exchange. Eleven seconds of her at full voltage, no hesitation, a life saved at the end of the world — let the unwashable stone be the best thing she ever did.

flag_worst.text:
You flag the nine seconds.

'Confirm,' the console asks, and you confirm, and somewhere below you, in the Principal's body, a span of the original day — 12:50:41 to 12:51:14, a woman at the foot of a drowned bridge, frozen, blank, terrified, choosing wrong with hate arriving live in her face — is set outside time's reach forever. No re-run will wash it. No reconciliation will smooth it. No future mercy, yours or the machine's or any iteration's, will ever be able to make her story kinder than it was. It is the single least flattering moment of her life, and it is now the most permanent object in the universe.

Osy watches you do it without a word. When the console seals the flag she sits back in the unpadded seat, and looks at the glass for a long time, and when she finally speaks it is not the flat voice; it is the one from the door of Hour 121, the one she uses for exactly one person, roughened to the grain:

'Every keeper in this story edits for the client. Memory keeps me shouting instead of her laughing. The Order keeps best evidence. The engine keeps me alive and arranged. Forty-seven men kept whatever let them climb down the rungs again.' Her hand comes over, and takes yours, on the console, over the sealed flag — the first time in eleven years, and the record keeps that too, and neither of you asks it not to. 'You just spent the only unwashable stone in the world on the nine seconds I would have paid anything to unhappen. Not my brilliance. Not my mercy. My freeze. The proof I was a person and not a protected square.' Her grip tightens once, hard, the way you close a hand on a coin tossed too hard. 'He wanted me to see him. You wanted me to be real. Colleague — I have been trying to find the difference between the men who wrote the ledger and the man beside me since the mirror room.' She lets go, and stands, and faces the inner door, and the tournament stillness comes down over her like a visor, but the voice under it has bedrock and daylight in it both: 'There it is. Open the door.'

item.name: The Unwashable Stone
item.description: 12:50:41 to 12:51:14, preserved outside every future edit: her freeze, her wrong branch, her terror — the proof she was a person and not a protected square. The most permanent object in the universe, and you spent it on her worst nine seconds.

choices.text: Take stock at the inner door — the household's last audit before the chair.

flag_best.text:
You flag the Corn Exchange.

'Confirm,' the console asks, and you confirm, and somewhere below you a span of the original day — eleven seconds, a stalled tram, a stranger's child handed up into a window by a woman who did not hesitate and did not wait for thanks — is set outside time's reach forever. The best thing she did on the worst day there was, unwashable now, a stone the river must always go around. It is right. It is defensible. It is what the eulogies would have chosen, and the archives, and any keeper in the world.

Osy watches the seal complete, and nods, once, and says, 'Thank you,' in the dictation voice, and means it — and then stands and faces the inner door, and you catch, in the glass's dying light, the thing her stillness does not quite cover: the smallest recalibration, weighed and shelved, the look of a woman who has been shown, one more time, gently, with love, by the best of all of them, which of her moments the keepers keep.

At the door she pauses, hand on B's head, and says it without turning, flat and quiet, entered into the record because everything is: 'For the file, colleague. The Exchange was already safe. Eleven million people saw the end of the world, but that child will remember the window her whole life — some things keep themselves. The nine seconds at the bridge, though.' A breath. 'Only the record had those. And the record belongs to the machine, and the machine edits for its client.' She scratches the dog's ears, once, and squares her shoulders at the door. 'Noted, not argued. You chose like a keeper. They all chose like keepers. Open the door.'

choices.text: Take stock at the inner door — the household's last audit before the chair.

waypoint_five.text:
The last audit but one convenes at the inner door itself, because there is nowhere further to convene: the reader waiting for its palm, the seam of the door fine as a hairline, and behind it, by the letter's account and the ledger's and the ward's, the chair, the Principal, and the choice forty-seven readings of you carried in already made.

The household takes stock the way it has taken stock since a folding table in a transit station: out loud, in order, sparing nothing. The day is watched, whole — the laugh, the freeze, the ash, the oath. The stone is set, unwashable, whichever stone this reading of you chose. The oath is spoken instead of buried — she'll see me, four years, forty-seven men, one sentence, out in the air at last where it cannot steer from the dark. Nine days remain on a god's countdown. And the door will open on the residual: the lines not computable from any corpus, p ≈ 0, where the games not yet played live, and where every interesting thing any examiner ever verified came out of.

B stands, stretches stem to stern, shakes himself once — ringing the invisible bell — and takes up station facing the inner door, tail neither high nor low: the whole paper read, the working stance, the household's third opinion delivered as posture.

Osy sets her back against the concrete beside the reader, arms folded, second's position, cutman's kit in her pocket — the endgame sheet, folded twice, exact — and looks at you across the last threshold of the long file, and asks it, one more time, the question that has built you audit by audit from a man with a legal pad to whatever is about to put its palm on that reader. Her voice keeps all its cold and all its bedrock and adds, at the bottom, for the first time at a waypoint, unmistakably, the other thing:

'Final calibration, colleague. The room is listening. What does this household need you to be?'
